title: Combating Houthi Threats and Aggression Act
short_title: Combating Houthi Threats and Aggression Act
plain_summary: This bill is about stopping the Houthis, a group that has been attacking ships in important waterways near Yemen. The President must punish people who help the Houthis attack these ships by putting sanctions on them. The President also has to send a report to Congress every year explaining what the Houthis are doing and how they are breaking rules about weapons. This law will last for five years and then stop working.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provi

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Combating Houthi Threats and Aggression Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Statement of Policy
sections.text: The United States will work with its allies and partners to keep shipping safe in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. This includes supporting efforts to stop attacks and opposing attacks on international shipping that threaten the free flow of trade, put sailors in danger, and break international law. These attacks hurt the world economy, make the Middle East and Africa less stable, and harm United States national security interests.

sections.title: Section 3 Report on Capability of the Houthis to Threaten United States National Security and Foreign Policy Goals
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, and every year after that, the President must send a report to certain congressional committees about how the Houthis (also called Ansar Allah) can threaten United States national security and foreign policy goals. The report must include an assessment of senior Houthi leaders' intentions and ability to conduct and continue military operations from Yemen that target the United States, Israel, or global shipping. It must describe the funding, materials, training, and other support the Houthis get from Iran, Hezbollah, or any other group acting for Iran, including help to improve their ability to make their own weapons and their existing weapons supply. The report must describe the Houthis' ballistic missiles and unmanned delivery systems, including those covered under Category One or Category Two of the Missile Control Technology Regime, and how precise and far-reaching these weapons are. It must describe the Houthis' sea-based capabilities, including sea missiles and drones. The report must analyze the Houthis' current ability to make their own weapons and how their control over the Al-Hudaydah port and Sana'a international airport helps them keep making weapons. It must assess the Houthis' stockpiles and use of commercial off-the-shelf dual-use drone technology and identify which countries these products come from. For this section, appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Armed Services of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Armed Services of the Senate.

sections.title: Section 4 Report on Houthi Attacks that Threaten Freedom of Navigation in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, and every year after that, the President must send a report to certain congressional committees that includes a summary of Houthi (also called Ansar Allah) attacks that threaten freedom of navigation in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. The report must assess how Houthi threats to freedom of navigation in these areas affect United States security interests. It must assess how these threats affect the global economy, including the United States economy. The report must assess Iran's role in the Houthis' attacks that threaten freedom of navigation in these areas, including Iran's help with targeting. It must describe China's presence in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden during the reporting period. The report must assess how the Houthis' attacks in these areas affect Russia's, China's, and Iran's freedom of navigation compared to the United States and its partners' freedom of navigation. The first report must cover the period from October seventh, two thousand twenty-three, to ninety days after this law is passed. Each following report must cover the one-year period after the previous report ended. For this section, appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Armed Services of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Armed Services of the Senate.

sections.title: Section 5 Report on Violations of the United Nations Arms Embargo Against Yemen
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, and every year after that, the President must send a report to certain congressional committees that describes violations or attempted violations of the United Nations arms embargo against Yemen authorized under United Nations Security Council Resolution twenty-two sixteen from two thousand fifteen and later resolutions. The report must list incidents where the United States or other countries stopped weapons and related parts believed to be going to the Houthis. For each stopping incident, the report must say what specific group conducted the stopping, the circumstances and location of the stopping, what weapons or parts were seized and where they likely came from, and under what authority each seizure happened. The report must describe what United States Government resources are currently used to identify, disrupt, stop, or otherwise break up the flow of illegal weapons intended for the Houthis, and identify under what authorities these activities happen. It must describe United States coordination with international partners on efforts to identify, disrupt, dismantle, or stop illegal weapons flows to the Houthis, including identifying United States security assistance and cooperation programs that help such partners' stopping efforts. The first report must cover the period from January first, two thousand twenty-two, to ninety days after this law is passed. Each following report must cover the one-year period after the previous report ended. For this section, appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Armed Services of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Armed Services of the Senate.

sections.title: Section 6 Sanctions with Respect to Any Attacks by the Houthis on International Shipping and Other Military Support to the Houthis
sections.text: The President must impose certain sanctions on any foreign person the President determines, on or after this law is passed, to be responsible for or involved in any attacks by the Houthis (also called Ansar Allah) that threaten international shipping in the Red Sea or Gulf of Aden. The sanctions also apply to anyone who knowingly engages in or tries to engage in activities or transactions that have significantly contributed to or pose a significant risk of contributing to such conduct. They also apply to anyone who knowingly engages in any activity that significantly contributes to supplying, selling, or transferring arms and related materials of all types, including weapons and ammunition, military vehicles and equipment, paramilitary equipment, and spare parts, as well as technical assistance, training, financial or other assistance, to any foreign person engaging in such conduct. The sanctions include blocking of property, meaning the President must use all authorities under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to block and prohibit all transactions involving the foreign person's property and interests in property if such property is in the United States, comes into the United States, or comes under the control of a United States person. The sanctions also make such people unable to get visas, admission, or parole into the United States. Any current visas of such people will be revoked immediately. Anyone who violates these sanctions will face the same penalties as those set out in the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. The President may waive these sanctions on a case-by-case basis for periods not exceeding one hundred eighty days only if the President determines and justifies in writing that the waiver is in the national security interests of the United States, and submits this determination to congressional committees at least fifteen days before the waiver takes effect. The President must brief congressional committees on the reasons for any waiver within sixty days of issuing it and every one hundred eighty days while it remains in effect. The President must issue regulations for implementing this section within one hundred twenty days of this law being passed and notify congressional committees at least ten days before issuing such regulations. These sanctions do not apply to intelligence activities subject to certain reporting requirements or authorized intelligence activities of the United States. They also do not apply if admitting someone into the United States is necessary to comply with international obligations regarding the United Nations headquarters agreement or to carry out authorized law enforcement activity in the United States.

sections.title: Section 7 Sunset
sections.text: This law will end five years after the date it is passed.
